content.text: Cross-Functional PRR Sign-Off Requirements

A Production Readiness Review for a Claude-based service touches domains that no single team owns. Engineering can verify retry logic and SLOs; only the security team can sign off on secret management and SBOM coverage; only legal can confirm that the data flows comply with the terms of service under which the Anthropic API is used. Cross-functional sign-off is the mechanism that ensures no domain is reviewed only by the team that built the feature.

Sign-off is not about bureaucracy. Each reviewer brings domain knowledge that the engineering team lacks. A security engineer will spot a secret-in-env-var that passed code review; a privacy reviewer will catch user data being sent to the API when a less granular alternative would suffice.

This sub-subtopic covers which functions must participate, what each is reviewing, how the review runs asynchronously without blocking launch, and how to handle disagreements and conditional approvals.

attrs.alt: Cross-functional PRR sign-off flow: engineering opens doc, five domain reviewers work in parallel, converge on gate, APPROVED or BLOCKED

content.text: Required Sign-Off Functions
Function
Domain reviewed
P0 criteria they own
SRE / Platform
Reliability, capacity, on-call coverage
Retry logic, circuit breaker, SLO definition, runbook existence, load test results
Security
Secret management, dependency risk, auth
API key in secrets manager, dependency CVE scan clear, SBOM generated, SLSA provenance
AI Safety / Policy
Output safety, content policy, adversarial robustness
Output filtering coverage, red-team results, prompt injection audit, PII not in system prompt
Legal / Privacy
Data usage compliance, retention
API ToS compliance for data category, user consent for AI processing, retention schedule
Finance / FinOps
Token budget, cost projections
Budget alert configured, per-request cost within model, cost projection for peak load
Two functions are optional depending on service characteristics:
Compliance (SOC 2 / HIPAA teams):
required if the service handles data in scope for a certification. Review covers audit logging completeness and evidence collection.
Product / UX:
required if Claude output is shown directly to end users. Review covers output tone, brand consistency, and user-facing error messages.
Review Timeline and Async Process
A blocking synchronous PRR meeting is appropriate only for the highest-risk launches. For most services, a well-structured async process delivers equivalent rigor without scheduling overhead:
T-14 days:
Engineering opens the PRR document, completes the self-assessed checklist, and tags all required reviewers.
T-14 to T-9 days:
Each reviewer reads the document and raises questions or P0 blockers as comments. Engineering resolves comments.
T-9 days:
Sync meeting (30 min) only if unresolved P0 blockers remain. Otherwise async sign-off continues.
T-5 days:
All reviewers provide explicit approval comment or conditional approval with named due date for open items.
T-0: launch
proceeds only if all P0 approvals are present. Non-P0 open items carry a post-launch due date in the scorecard.

The document owner (typically the engineering lead) is responsible for chasing reviewers who have not responded within their SLA. Reviewers who miss the deadline without raising a blocker are treated as implicitly approved — silence is not a veto.
PRR Document Structure
A single Google Doc or Notion page serves as the canonical artifact. It must include, in order:

PRR: <Service Name> — <Launch Date>
Owner: <engineering lead email>
Reviewers:
  - SRE: [name]
  - Security: [name]
  - AI Safety: [name]
  - Legal/Privacy: [name]
  - Finance: [name]
Target launch: YYYY-MM-DD

## 1. Service Description (3–5 sentences)
## 2. Traffic & Data Characteristics
   - Peak RPS estimate
   - Data sensitivity class (public / internal / confidential / regulated)
   - User population
## 3. Architecture Diagram (link or embedded)
## 4. Checklist (by domain — see PRR Checklist sub-subtopic)
## 5. Open Items (non-P0, with owner and due date)
## 6. Sign-Off Table (populated by reviewers)

Sign-Off Table:
| Function       | Reviewer     | Status   | Date       | Notes |
|---------------|-------------|----------|------------|-------|
| SRE           | Alice Smith  | APPROVED |            |       |
| Security      | Bob Jones    | COND-APPR| 2026-07-08 | SBOM gate: open |
| AI Safety     | Carol Davis  | APPROVED |            |       |
| Legal/Privacy | Dan Wilson   | APPROVED |            |       |
| Finance       | Eve Brown    | APPROVED |            |       |
What Each Reviewer Looks For
SRE Review
SRE reviewers focus on runtime behavior under load and failure. They will ask for evidence, not assertions:
Show me the load test report — not the config, the results.
Where is the circuit breaker config? What is the threshold and what does the fallback return?
Open the runbook. Can an on-call engineer execute it at 2am without context?
What is the SLO error budget today, and how does this service consume it?
Security Review
Security reviewers look for systemic risk, not just code review:
Run a grep for the API key pattern in the repo. Show me it is absent.
Attach the Grype scan report. Are there CRITICAL findings? If so, what is the mitigation or exception?
Where is the SBOM? Is it CycloneDX or SPDX? Is it stored with the build artifact?
How is the API key rotated? Who can initiate rotation? What is the SLA if rotation fails?
AI Safety / Policy Review
AI Safety reviewers assess whether the service's use of Claude is within policy and whether the output safety mechanisms are adequate for the user population:
What is the system prompt? Does it constrain the model's role appropriately?
Is user input passed to the API? How is it validated or sanitized?
What red-team tests were run? Were any jailbreaks or injection attacks successful?
If the service shows Claude output to users, what is the process when a user receives harmful content?
Is the output schema validated? What happens when the model returns malformed output?
Legal / Privacy Review
Legal reviewers verify that the service's data flows are consistent with the Anthropic ToS, applicable privacy law, and user consent:
What data categories are sent to the Anthropic API? (User messages, PII, IP addresses, health data?)
Is user consent obtained for AI processing? Where is it documented?
What is the data retention policy for Claude API request/response logs?
Does the workspace ToS allow this use case? (B2C consumer services have additional constraints vs. internal tooling.)
Conditional Approvals
A conditional approval means: approved if and only if the named items are resolved by the stated date. It is distinct from a full approval and from a blocker.

# Example conditional approval entry in the sign-off table
function: Security
reviewer: bob.jones@example.com
status: CONDITIONAL_APPROVED
conditions:
  - item: SBOM uploaded to artifact registry
    due: "2026-07-08"
    evidence_required: "S3 URI of the CycloneDX JSON for the production build"
  - item: Grype scan re-run after dependency update in PR #431
    due: "2026-07-08"
    evidence_required: "CI run link showing scan passed"
date_of_approval_if_conditions_met: null   # filled by reviewer when evidence provided
notes: |
  Main concerns addressed. SBOM and re-scan are mechanical — approve now to unblock
  launch prep. I will confirm full approval when evidence is provided.

The document owner tracks conditional approval items on the same open-items list as non-P0 checklist items. Launch is permitted only after all conditional approval conditions are satisfied and the reviewer has updated their status to APPROVED.
Handling Disagreements
When a reviewer raises a blocker that engineering believes is wrong or disproportionate, the resolution process must be explicit:
Engineering acknowledges the concern in writing
in the PRR document within 24 hours.
Engineering provides a technical rebuttal or proposes an equivalent control.
The rebuttal must be specific — not "we think the risk is low" but "the risk is mitigated by X because Y."
Reviewer responds within 24 hours.
If they withdraw the blocker, they update the sign-off table. If they maintain it, escalation proceeds.
Escalation:
engineering and reviewer each nominate one peer from their function. The four discuss and produce a written resolution within 48 hours.
Executive decision:
if the four cannot agree, the decision escalates to the VPs of both functions. The VP decision is final and documented in the PRR.

The goal of escalation is not to override the reviewer — it is to get the right decision with the right context. Reviewers who raise valid concerns that are overridden should document their objection in the PRR so it is visible in post-incident analysis if the risk materializes.
Post-Launch Closure
The PRR is not complete at launch. Open items committed at sign-off must be resolved and evidenced in the document:

# Post-launch section appended to PRR doc after launch
post_launch:
  launch_date: "2026-07-08"
  open_items_closed:
    - item: SBOM uploaded
      closed_date: "2026-07-07"
      evidence: "s3://acme-prompt-registry/builds/triage-api/v2.1.0/sbom.json"
      closed_by: bob.jones@example.com

    - item: Grype scan re-run
      closed_date: "2026-07-07"
      evidence: "https://github.com/org/triage-api/actions/runs/123456"
      closed_by: bob.jones@example.com

  security_final_approval: "2026-07-07"
  prr_closed: "2026-07-07"
A PRR with unclosed open items past their due date is an incident risk. Engineering leads are responsible for closing items on time; SREs are responsible for escalating if they are not.
Lighter Process for Low-Risk Services
Not every Claude service requires the full five-function review. A tiered model reduces overhead for lower-risk launches:
Tier
Service characteristics
Required sign-offs
Max timeline
Tier 1 (Full PRR)
Customer-facing, regulated data, new model version, > 100 RPS
SRE + Security + AI Safety + Legal + Finance
14 days
Tier 2 (Streamlined)
Internal tooling, no PII, existing model version
SRE + Security + AI Safety
7 days
Tier 3 (Lightweight)
Developer sandbox, no external users, < 10 RPS
SRE only + engineering self-review
2 days

Tier assignment is made by the engineering lead and must be confirmed (not just accepted) by the SRE reviewer. Mis-tiering to avoid review is grounds for a mandatory post-mortem regardless of incident outcome.
Tooling: PRR Document as Code

Storing the PRR as a YAML file in Git (alongside deploy configs) enables automated checks, change tracking, and integration with the deployment pipeline. The deployment gate refuses to promote to production unless the PRR document for the service version is present and all P0 fields are set to APPROVED.
Store PRR YAML files under
Store PRR YAML files under
docs/prr/<service>-<version>.yaml
in the same repository as the service. The file becomes part of the pull request that bumps the production version, so reviewers can see the PRR and the code change in the same diff.
Common PRR Failure Patterns
Understanding which PRR items are most often raised as blockers helps engineering teams front-load the right work:
Failure pattern
Function that raises it
Prevention
API key committed in .env file or docker-compose.yml
Security
Run secret-scanning CI (trufflehog/gitleaks) on every PR
No timeout on messages.create — uses library default (10 min)
SRE
ESLint/pylint rule that flags missing timeout= in API calls
System prompt contains user-supplied text via f-string
AI Safety
Static analysis script run in CI (see PRR Checklist sub-subtopic)
No eval suite for the production prompt version
AI Safety
Require eval_suite field in prompt.yaml schema validation
User PII sent to the API without documented justification
Legal/Privacy
Data flow diagram mandatory in PRR doc template
No monthly token budget alert in Anthropic Console
Finance
Onboarding checklist: console alert setup required before staging deploy
Load test not run — "we expect < 10 RPS" asserted without evidence
SRE
Require load test result link in PRR capacity section
Summary
Five required functions:
SRE, Security, AI Safety/Policy, Legal/Privacy, and Finance/FinOps each own specific P0 criteria that are not delegatable to the engineering team.
Async by default:
async review with a 14-day runway avoids blocking synchronous meetings; a sync call convenes only if unresolved P0 blockers remain at T-9 days.
Conditional approvals:
allow launch prep to proceed while mechanical items are resolved; named conditions with evidence requirements, not vague "follow-ups".
Disagreement process:
written rebuttals, peer escalation, then VP decision — all documented in the PRR so post-incident analysis has the full context.
Post-launch closure:
the PRR remains open until all committed open items are evidenced and the final sign-off is recorded.
Tiered model:
match review intensity to service risk; require SRE confirmation of tier assignment to prevent mis-tiering.
